claim.hypotheses: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas due to reputation concerns.
claim.statement: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas.

data.source: Participants recruited from a larger pool of volunteers for a moral cognition study.
data.notes: Participants were screened for prior exposure to moral dilemma literature and advanced psychology courses.

method.description: Participants completed moral dilemma tasks with and without observers present to measure the effect of social observation on moral judgment.
steps: Recruit participants and conduct power analysis.
Conduct pretest to measure baseline moral decision tendencies.
Randomly assign participants to observed or control group.
Conduct main task with moral dilemmas and social observation manipulation.
Conduct lexical decision task to measure effect of social observation.
Analyze data using mixed ANOVA and other statistical tools.
method.control_variables: Baseline moral decision tendencies, individual psychological traits

results.summary: Social observation increased deontological judgments, with decreased decision confidence and prolonged reaction times.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Deontological judgment in personal moral dilemmas
numerical_results.value: 63.96% for observed group vs. 52.29% for control group

metadata.original_paper_title: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas
